facts.text: Wonder Woman first appeared in All Star Comics #8 in December 1941, created by psychologist William Moulton Marston and artist Harry G. Peter.
William Moulton Marston created Wonder Woman under the pen name Charles Moulton, a combination of his and comics editor Max Gaines's middle names.
Marston was inspired to create Wonder Woman by his wife Elizabeth Holloway Marston and their partner Olive Byrne, who both served as models for the character.
William Moulton Marston was a psychologist who invented the systolic blood pressure test, a key component of the modern polygraph — which directly inspired Wonder Woman's Lasso of Truth.
Wonder Woman's real name is Diana, Princess of Themyscira, and she often uses the alias Diana Prince in the mortal world.
In her original origin story, Diana was sculpted from clay by her mother Hippolyta and brought to life by the Greek gods.
The New 52 reboot in 2011 changed Diana's origin so that she was the biological daughter of Hippolyta and Zeus, king of the Greek gods.
Wonder Woman received her own solo comic series, Sensation Comics, starting in January 1942, just one month after her debut in All Star Comics.
Wonder Woman was one of the founding members of the Justice Society of America, though she initially served as the team's secretary — a reflection of 1940s gender norms.
Paradise Island, later renamed Themyscira, is the hidden home of the Amazons where Diana was raised, free from the influence of the outside world.
The Amazons of Themyscira are immortal warrior women granted eternal life by the Greek gods as long as they remain on their island.
"I am Diana of Themyscira, daughter of Hippolyta, Queen of the Amazons. In the name of all that is good, your wrath upon this world is over." — Wonder Woman's declaration to Ares in the 2017 film.
Wonder Woman's iconic tiara is not just decorative — it can be used as a razor-sharp boomerang weapon in combat.
The Bracelets of Submission worn by Wonder Woman are indestructible and can deflect bullets, energy blasts, and virtually any projectile.
In the original comics, if a man welded Wonder Woman's bracelets together, she would lose her Amazonian strength — a concept later abandoned.
Wonder Woman's Lasso of Truth, also called the Golden Perfect, compels anyone bound by it to tell the absolute truth.
The Lasso of Truth is forged from the Golden Girdle of Gaea and is completely unbreakable in most comic continuities.
Wonder Woman's invisible jet, also known as the Invisible Plane, was her primary mode of long-distance travel before she was given the power of flight in post-Crisis continuity.
"It's not about deserve. It's about what you believe. And I believe in love." — Diana's iconic line to Ares in Wonder Woman (2017).
Steve Trevor, a U.S. Army intelligence officer, is Wonder Woman's primary love interest and was the first man Diana ever encountered.
In the original comics, Steve Trevor crash-landed on Paradise Island, prompting the Amazons to hold a tournament to determine who would escort him back to 'Man's World.'
Diana won the tournament to leave Themyscira by competing against her fellow Amazons in disguise, as Hippolyta had forbidden her from entering.
Wonder Woman has been a member of the Justice League since the team's earliest incarnations and is considered one of DC's 'Trinity' alongside Batman and Superman.
The DC Trinity — Superman, Batman, and Wonder Woman — represent hope, justice, and truth, respectively.
"I used to want to save the world. To end all wars and bring peace to mankind. But then I glimpsed the darkness that lives within their light." — Diana in Wonder Woman (2017).
Ares, the Greek god of war, is one of Wonder Woman's most formidable and recurring villains in the comics.
Cheetah, whose most well-known incarnation is Dr. Barbara Ann Minerva, is Wonder Woman's arch-nemesis and has appeared in numerous comic runs.
Circe, the sorceress from Greek mythology, is a major Wonder Woman villain who has repeatedly tried to destroy the Amazons and conquer the world.
Doctor Poison, one of Wonder Woman's earliest villains, first appeared in Sensation Comics #2 in 1942 and was portrayed by Elena Anaya in the 2017 film.
Maxwell Lord is a powerful businessman and telepath who became one of Wonder Woman's most controversial enemies when she killed him to save Superman in 2005.
In the controversial 'Sacrifice' storyline, Wonder Woman snapped Maxwell Lord's neck on live television to free Superman from Lord's mind control, leading to a public relations crisis for Diana.
Giganta is a size-changing supervillain who frequently battles Wonder Woman, capable of growing to enormous heights while gaining proportional strength.

"We have a saying, my people. Don't kill if you can wound, don't wound if you can subdue, don't subdue if you can pacify, and don't raise your hand at all until you've first extended it." — Wonder Woman in the comics.
Wonder Woman's golden armor, known as the Eagle Armor or Golden Eagle Armor, has appeared in several comic storylines and was featured prominently in Wonder Woman 1984.
William Moulton Marston lived in a polyamorous relationship with his wife Elizabeth Holloway Marston and Olive Byrne, and both women helped shape Wonder Woman's character.
Elizabeth Holloway Marston was a feminist, lawyer, and psychologist whose strength and independence were key inspirations for Wonder Woman.
Olive Byrne wore distinctive wide bracelets that directly inspired Wonder Woman's Bracelets of Submission.
Marston believed that women were inherently more honest and reliable than men, and he designed Wonder Woman to be a feminist role model for young girls.
"Not even girls want to be girls so long as our feminine archetype lacks force, strength, and power." — William Moulton Marston, explaining why he created Wonder Woman.
After William Moulton Marston died in 1947, Robert Kanigher took over writing Wonder Woman and shifted the character away from Marston's feminist themes.
In the late 1960s, writer Denny O'Neil stripped Wonder Woman of her powers and costume, reimagining her as a martial arts-trained adventurer named Diana Prince — a controversial era.
Gloria Steinem put Wonder Woman on the cover of the first regular issue of Ms. Magazine in 1972, helping restore the character's feminist icon status.
The United Nations named Wonder Woman an Honorary Ambassador for the Empowerment of Women and Girls in 2016, though the appointment was controversially rescinded two months later.
George Pérez relaunched Wonder Woman in 1987 with a post-Crisis on Infinite Earths reboot that deeply rooted the character in Greek mythology.
George Pérez's Wonder Woman run is widely regarded as the definitive version of the character, establishing her as an ambassador of peace from Themyscira.
In George Pérez's reboot, the Amazons were reincarnated souls of women who had been murdered by men throughout history.
"The Gods gave the Amazons a sacred duty — to spread the message of peace and equality to Patriarch's World." — from George Pérez's Wonder Woman run.
Greg Rucka's first run on Wonder Woman (2003-2006) explored Diana as a political figure and ambassador, focusing on diplomacy and moral complexity.
In Greg Rucka's run, Wonder Woman published a book called 'Reflections' to spread Amazonian philosophy to the outside world.
Greg Rucka returned to write Wonder Woman in DC Rebirth (2016), creating the acclaimed 'Year One' and 'The Lies' storylines.
In Greg Rucka's Rebirth run, he confirmed that Wonder Woman is queer, stating that on an island of only women, the concept of gay wouldn't exist as they wouldn't have a word for it.
Gail Simone wrote Wonder Woman from 2008 to 2010, bringing her trademark wit and character-driven storytelling to the series.
Gail Simone introduced Genocide, a powerful villain created from soil soaked in the blood of victims from the world's worst genocides, during her Wonder Woman run.
"Who is Wonder Woman? She is the best of all of us. She is the dream behind the dream of what we all hope to be." — from Gail Simone's Wonder Woman.
Phil Jimenez, often called the 'male George Pérez,' had a celebrated run on Wonder Woman in the early 2000s that was heavily influenced by Pérez's work.
Brian Azzarello and Cliff Chiang's New 52 Wonder Woman run reimagined the Greek gods in modern settings and made Diana a daughter of Zeus.
In the Brian Azzarello run, Wonder Woman became the God of War after defeating Ares, inheriting his title and responsibilities.
Lynda Carter starred as Wonder Woman in the TV series that ran from 1975 to 1979, becoming the definitive live-action portrayal for decades.
The Lynda Carter Wonder Woman series premiered on ABC as 'The New Original Wonder Woman' set during World War II, before moving to CBS as 'The New Adventures of Wonder Woman' set in the present day.
Lynda Carter's iconic spin transformation — where Diana Prince twirls to become Wonder Woman in an explosion of light — became one of the most recognizable scenes in television history.
"All the world is waiting for you, and the power you possess. In your satin tights, fighting for your rights, and the old red, white, and blue!" — the Wonder Woman TV series theme song.
Lyle Waggoner played Steve Trevor in the Lynda Carter Wonder Woman series, first as Steve Trevor Sr. in the WWII-era season, then as Steve Trevor Jr. in the modern-day seasons.
Before Lynda Carter, a 1974 TV movie starred Cathy Lee Crosby as a blonde, non-superpowered Wonder Woman — it was poorly received and quickly forgotten.
Lynda Carter was crowned Miss World America in 1972 before being cast as Wonder Woman, and she performed her own stunts on the show.
The 1975-1979 Wonder Woman series ran for 59 episodes across three seasons and remains a beloved piece of superhero television history.
"I am Wonder Woman. I can do what I want." — Lynda Carter in a classic TV moment.
A Wonder Woman TV pilot starring Adrianne Palicki was produced by David E. Kelley for NBC in 2011 but was never picked up for a series.
Wonder Woman (2017), directed by Patty Jenkins and starring Gal Gadot, was released on June 2, 2017, and became a massive critical and commercial success.
Gal Gadot was cast as Wonder Woman in 2013 for her debut in Batman v Superman: Dawn of Justice (2016), beating out numerous other actresses.
Gal Gadot served two years in the Israel Defense Forces as a combat fitness instructor before pursuing acting, which helped prepare her for the physical demands of playing Diana.
"I will fight for those who cannot fight for themselves." — Diana Prince in Wonder Woman (2017).
Patty Jenkins became the first woman to direct a major superhero film with a budget over $100 million when she helmed Wonder Woman (2017).
Wonder Woman (2017) grossed over $821 million worldwide, making it the highest-grossing film in the DCEU at the time of its release.
Wonder Woman (2017) received a 93% approval rating on Rotten Tomatoes, making it one of the best-reviewed superhero films ever.
The No Man's Land sequence in Wonder Woman (2017), where Diana crosses a WWI battlefield alone, is often cited as one of the greatest moments in superhero cinema.
Patty Jenkins has stated that the No Man's Land scene was nearly cut from the film by studio executives who didn't understand its importance.
Chris Pine played Steve Trevor in Wonder Woman (2017) and Wonder Woman 1984, bringing humor and heart to the role.
"I can save today. You can save the world." — Steve Trevor's farewell to Diana in Wonder Woman (2017).
Robin Wright played General Antiope, Diana's aunt and the greatest warrior of the Amazons, in Wonder Woman (2017).
"You are stronger than you believe. You have greater power than you know." — Antiope's final words to Diana in Wonder Woman (2017).
Connie Nielsen portrayed Queen Hippolyta, Diana's mother and ruler of the Amazons, in Wonder Woman (2017) and subsequent DCEU films.
"Be careful in the world of men, Diana. They do not deserve you." — Hippolyta's warning to Diana in Wonder Woman (2017).
Danny Huston played Erich Ludendorff in Wonder Woman (2017), a fictionalized version of the real German general from World War I.
David Thewlis was cast as Sir Patrick Morgan, who is ultimately revealed to be Ares, the god of war, in Wonder Woman (2017).
"I am not your enemy. I am the God of Truth, not War." — Ares attempting to persuade Diana in Wonder Woman (2017).
Saïd Taghmaoui, Ewen Bremner, and Eugene Brave Rock played Steve Trevor's ragtag team of allies — Sameer, Charlie, and Chief — in Wonder Woman (2017).
Wonder Woman 1984, directed by Patty Jenkins, was released on December 25, 2020, simultaneously in theaters and on HBO Max due to the COVID-19 pandemic.
Wonder Woman 1984 is set during the Cold War and features Diana facing off against Maxwell Lord and the Cheetah.
Pedro Pascal played Maxwell Lord in Wonder Woman 1984, portraying him as a desperate businessman seeking ultimate power through a magical wishing stone.
"Life is good, but it can be better!" — Maxwell Lord's catchphrase in Wonder Woman 1984.
Kristen Wiig played Dr. Barbara Ann Minerva, who transforms into the villainous Cheetah, in Wonder Woman 1984.
Wonder Woman 1984 featured Diana using the Golden Eagle Armor, a powerful suit of winged golden armor inspired by the comics.
"Nothing good is born from lies. And greatness is not what you think." — Diana Prince in Wonder Woman 1984.
Wonder Woman 1984 grossed $170 million worldwide, far less than its predecessor, partly due to pandemic-era restrictions.
The Dreamstone in Wonder Woman 1984 is based on the concept of the monkey's paw — granting wishes but extracting a heavy price.
Chris Pine returned as Steve Trevor in Wonder Woman 1984 through a controversial resurrection plotline involving the Dreamstone.
Gal Gadot first appeared as Wonder Woman in Batman v Superman: Dawn of Justice (2016), directed by Zack Snyder.
Wonder Woman's entrance in Batman v Superman, accompanied by her electric cello theme composed by Hans Zimmer and Junkie XL, was one of the most crowd-pleasing moments of the film.
The Wonder Woman electric cello theme, featuring cellist Tina Guo, became one of the most recognizable superhero musical motifs in modern cinema.
"I've killed things from other worlds before." — Diana to Bruce Wayne in Batman v Superman: Dawn of Justice (2016).
Gal Gadot appeared as Wonder Woman in Justice League (2017) and Zack Snyder's Justice League (2021), serving as a key member of the team.
In Zack Snyder's Justice League, Wonder Woman has an expanded role and backstory, including a sequence where she thwarts a terrorist attack in London.
"I belong to no one." — Wonder Woman in Zack Snyder's Justice League (2021).
Gal Gadot also made a cameo as Wonder Woman in The Flash (2023) and Shazam! Fury of the Gods (2023).
Wonder Woman has appeared in numerous DC animated films, including Justice League: War, Wonder Woman: Bloodlines, and Justice League Dark: Apokolips War.
The 2009 animated film Wonder Woman, featuring the voice of Keri Russell as Diana, is considered one of the best DC animated movies ever made.
In the 2009 animated Wonder Woman film, Nathan Fillion voiced Steve Trevor and Alfred Molina voiced Ares.
Virginia Madsen voiced Hippolyta in the 2009 animated Wonder Woman film, bringing regal authority to the Amazon queen.
Wonder Woman has been a regular character in the Justice League and Justice League Unlimited animated series (2001-2006), voiced by Susan Eisenberg.
Susan Eisenberg has voiced Wonder Woman more times than any other actress, spanning multiple animated series, films, and video games over two decades.
In the Justice League animated series, Wonder Woman's relationship with Batman was a recurring romantic subplot that fans passionately debated.
"Don't I look like a woman who can take care of herself?" — Wonder Woman in Justice League Unlimited.
Wonder Woman appeared in the Super Friends animated series in the 1970s and 1980s, voiced by Shannon Farnon.
In DC Super Hero Girls, Wonder Woman is reimagined as a teenager attending Super Hero High School, making the character accessible to younger audiences.
Wonder Woman has been a playable character in every Injustice fighting game, including Injustice: Gods Among Us (2013) and Injustice 2 (2017).
In the Injustice universe, Wonder Woman is a morally complex character who sides with Superman's authoritarian Regime, serving as one of the game's antagonists.
Wonder Woman is a playable character in DC Universe Online, the MMORPG that has been running since 2011.
Wonder Woman has appeared as a LEGO minifigure in multiple LEGO DC sets and in LEGO DC Super-Villains and LEGO Batman video games.
A standalone Wonder Woman video game developed by Monolith Productions (creators of the Middle-earth: Shadow of Mordor series) was announced in 2021.
Wonder Woman has been featured in the mobile game DC Legends and the digital card game DC Dual Force.
"The world needs Wonder Woman." — tagline from the Wonder Woman (2017) marketing campaign.
Wonder Woman's costume has evolved significantly over the decades, from a star-spangled skirt in the 1940s to the iconic bustier and hot pants, and finally to more armor-inspired designs.
The original Wonder Woman costume was inspired by the American flag, reflecting the patriotic themes of World War II-era comics.
In the New 52, Wonder Woman's costume was redesigned with a more armored look, including a silver color scheme instead of the traditional gold.
Wonder Woman's star-spangled shorts were replaced with a full-length skirt or battle skirt in several modern comic runs, sparking debate among fans.
"You know, it's not about what you deserve. It's about what you believe." — Steve Trevor in Wonder Woman (2017).
Wonder Woman is one of the longest continuously published comic book characters, with her series running almost uninterrupted since 1942.
Wonder Woman #750, published in 2020, celebrated the character's legacy with contributions from numerous acclaimed writers and artists.
The Amazons of Themyscira are trained in every form of combat, making them some of the most skilled warriors in the DC Universe.
In the comics, the Amazons were originally enslaved by Hercules before breaking free and being granted Themyscira by the gods as a sanctuary.
Hippolyta, Diana's mother, has occasionally taken on the Wonder Woman mantle herself, including a stint with the Justice Society of America.
Donna Troy, also known as Wonder Girl and later Troia, was the first protégé of Wonder Woman and has one of the most complicated backstories in DC Comics.
Cassie Sandsmark, the second Wonder Girl, is the daughter of Zeus and the archaeologist Helena Sandsmark, and she served with the Teen Titans.
Nubia, an Amazon warrior and Diana's sister, has become an increasingly prominent character in DC Comics, starring in her own series 'Nubia and the Amazons.'
Artemis of Bana-Mighdall is a fierce Amazon warrior who briefly served as Wonder Woman in the 1990s when Diana was stripped of the title.
"Peace is a virtue. A state of mind, a disposition for benevolence, confidence, and justice. It is not simply the absence of war." — Wonder Woman in the comics.
Wonder Woman possesses superhuman strength, speed, durability, and flight — placing her on par with Superman as one of the most powerful heroes in the DC Universe.
Diana's powers include enhanced senses, accelerated healing, and resistance to magic, making her effective against both physical and mystical threats.
In addition to her superpowers, Wonder Woman is one of the most skilled hand-to-hand combatants in the DC Universe, trained since childhood by the Amazons.
Wonder Woman has been depicted wielding a sword and shield in modern comics and films, adding to her warrior image beyond the lasso and bracelets.
The God Killer sword, featured in the 2017 film, was believed to be a weapon capable of slaying a god — though Diana herself turns out to be the true 'god killer.'
"I am the man who can!" — Ares in Wonder Woman (2017), boasting about his power before Diana defeats him.
Wonder Woman's strength has been shown to rival Superman's in multiple comic storylines, and she has even bested him in combat on several occasions.
In the 'Hiketeia' graphic novel by Greg Rucka, Wonder Woman swears an oath to protect a young woman, putting her in direct conflict with Batman.
Wonder Woman has lifted Thor's hammer Mjolnir in the DC/Marvel crossover comics, proving her worthiness.
In the JLA: A League of One storyline, Wonder Woman single-handedly defeats every member of the Justice League to protect them from a prophesied dragon.

"If the prospect of living in a world where trying to respect the basic rights of those around you and valuing each other simply because we exist is such a daunting, impossible task, then what sort of world are we left with?" — Wonder Woman in the comics.
The 2017 Wonder Woman film is set during World War I, unlike the original comics which placed her debut during World War II.
Patty Jenkins chose to set the film during World War I because it was 'the first time we had mechanized war and truly faceless, massive destruction.'
The island of Themyscira in the 2017 film was filmed on location in southern Italy, including the stunning Amalfi Coast and Matera.
The Amazon training sequences in Wonder Woman (2017) were filmed partly in England and partly in Italy, featuring real athletes and stunt performers.
Many of the Amazons in the 2017 film were played by professional athletes, including boxer Ann Wolfe, CrossFit champion Brooke Ence, and MMA fighter Madeleine Vall Beijner.
Gal Gadot was five months pregnant during reshoots for Wonder Woman (2017), with the crew using green screen to conceal her baby bump.
"What one does when faced with the truth is more difficult than you'd think." — Diana Prince in Wonder Woman (2017).
The Wonder Woman (2017) film was the first female-led superhero film since Elektra (2005) and Catwoman (2004), and far more successful than either.
Wonder Woman (2017) opened to $103.3 million domestically, setting a record for a female-directed film at the time.
The film inspired the 'Wonder Woman effect' — a documented spike in girls expressing interest in superhero play and costumes after its release.
Wonder Woman 1984's opening scene features a young Diana competing in an Amazon games tournament on Themyscira, an homage to the original comic origin.
Lilly Aspell played young Diana in both Wonder Woman (2017) and Wonder Woman 1984, performing many of her own stunts.
"You cannot be the winner, because you are not ready to win. And there is no shame in that." — Antiope to young Diana in Wonder Woman 1984.
Wonder Woman 1984 draws inspiration from the Dreamstone and Monkey's Paw narratives, exploring themes of sacrifice and the cost of desire.
The opening mall fight scene in Wonder Woman 1984 was partly an homage to 1980s action films and the era's consumer culture.
Lynda Carter made a cameo appearance in Wonder Woman 1984's post-credits scene as Asteria, an ancient Amazon warrior.
"I've always been ahead of my time." — Asteria (Lynda Carter) in Wonder Woman 1984's post-credits scene.
The Asteria character referenced in Wonder Woman 1984 is named after the Titan goddess of falling stars in Greek mythology.
Hans Zimmer composed the full score for Wonder Woman 1984 after co-creating Wonder Woman's theme for Batman v Superman.
Wonder Woman was ranked #5 on IGN's list of the 'Top 100 Comic Book Heroes of All Time.'
Wonder Woman was ranked #6 on Empire magazine's list of the '50 Greatest Comic Book Characters.'
In 2011, Wonder Woman was ranked #20 on IGN's 'Top 100 Super Heroes' list — though many fans argued she deserved a higher placement.
"If loss makes you doubt your belief in justice, then you never truly believed in justice at all." — Wonder Woman in the comics.
Wonder Woman has appeared on the cover of more comic books than any other female superhero in history.
DC Comics issue #600 of Wonder Woman in 2010 featured a controversial costume redesign by Jim Lee that gave Diana a jacket and pants.
The Jim Lee redesign of Wonder Woman's costume was met with mixed reactions and was eventually abandoned in favor of more traditional looks.
Wonder Woman's invisible jet was originally introduced because, in her early comics, she could not fly — it was later phased out when she gained the power of flight.
In some continuity, Wonder Woman's invisible jet is actually a sentient alien being that can shapeshift, not merely an invisible aircraft.
"Feeding people is the greatest act of peace and kindness." — Wonder Woman, reflecting her compassionate philosophy.
The character of Etta Candy, Wonder Woman's best friend, has been a part of the comics since the 1940s as a fun-loving, brave comic relief character.
Lucy Davis played Etta Candy in Wonder Woman (2017), providing comic relief as Steve Trevor's devoted secretary.
"I'm both frightened and aroused." — Etta Candy (Lucy Davis) in Wonder Woman (2017).
Wonder Woman's origin was influenced by Greek mythology, particularly the myths of the Amazons — female warriors who, in myth, lived apart from men.
The historical Amazons described by ancient Greek writers like Herodotus may have been inspired by Scythian warrior women of the Central Asian steppes.
"In a world of ordinary mortals, you are a Wonder Woman." — from the 1975 TV series theme song.
Wonder Woman is an honorary member of the Green Lantern Corps in some storylines, having briefly wielded a Star Sapphire ring as well.
During the Blackest Night crossover event, Wonder Woman was briefly converted into a Star Sapphire, wielding the violet ring of love.
In the 'Odyssey' storyline (2010-2011) by J. Michael Straczynski, Wonder Woman's timeline was altered, giving her a completely different history and costume.
"We are all connected. What hurts one of us hurts all of us." — Wonder Woman, emphasizing her belief in collective humanity.
The 'Circle' storyline by Gail Simone revealed a group of four Amazon guards who had conspired against baby Diana, believing her birth was an affront to Amazonian tradition.
Wonder Woman has diplomatic immunity in the DC Universe, as she serves as the Themysciran ambassador to the United Nations.
In the 'Eyes of the Gorgon' storyline by Greg Rucka, Wonder Woman was blinded by Medusa but continued fighting using her other heightened senses.
Wonder Woman killed Medusa on live television in Greg Rucka's run, blinding herself to avoid the Gorgon's petrifying gaze, then beheading the monster.
"I came here to parley. But do not mistake a desire for peace with a lack of preparation for war." — Wonder Woman in the comics.
William Moulton Marston originally intended Wonder Woman to be a vehicle for his theories about submission and dominance, which he believed could create a better society.
Marston's DISC theory of human behavior — Dominance, Inducement, Submission, and Compliance — directly influenced Wonder Woman's themes of loving submission and truth.
The bondage imagery in early Wonder Woman comics — chains, ropes, and submission — was intentional by Marston, reflecting his psychological theories.
Olive Byrne, who lived with the Marstons in a polyamorous relationship, was the niece of birth control pioneer Margaret Sanger, connecting Wonder Woman's origins to the feminist movement.
The book 'The Secret History of Wonder Woman' by Jill Lepore (2014) extensively documents the connections between Marston's personal life and Wonder Woman's creation.
"Wonder Woman is psychological propaganda for the new type of woman who, I believe, should rule the world." — William Moulton Marston, 1943.
After Marston's death, the comics largely abandoned his feminist and psychological themes, and Wonder Woman became a more conventional romance-adventure comic.
The 'I Ching' era (1968-1972) saw Diana lose her powers, give up her costume, and become a mod-styled adventurer trained in martial arts by a blind Asian mentor named I Ching.
Feminist icon Gloria Steinem was instrumental in restoring Wonder Woman's classic costume and powers, using her platform at Ms. Magazine to advocate for the character.

"Look, a beautiful young girl with long legs and black hair appears to be flying through the air at a tremendous rate of speed." — the first narrative description of Wonder Woman in All Star Comics #8.
H.G. Peter, the original Wonder Woman artist, had a distinctive art style that gave the early comics a unique charm that set them apart from other superhero titles.
Wonder Woman was one of only three DC superheroes (along with Superman and Batman) to maintain continuous publication throughout the 1950s when superhero comics declined.
In the Silver Age of Comics, Wonder Woman's stories became increasingly fantastical, featuring alien invasions and bizarre transformations.
Mike Sekowsky took over Wonder Woman in the late 1960s and radically reinvented the character, stripping away her powers and Amazonian heritage.
"The beauty of loving someone is that when everything changes, your love for that someone holds you steady." — Wonder Woman in the comics.
Wonder Woman #329 (1986) was the final issue before Crisis on Infinite Earths reset the character, ending Diana's pre-Crisis continuity.
George Pérez drew and co-wrote Wonder Woman for the first 62 issues of the post-Crisis relaunch, an impressive creative run.
Pérez's Wonder Woman depicted the Greek gods as active participants in Diana's story, with characters like Ares, Hermes, and Athena playing major roles.
In Pérez's continuity, Wonder Woman could not fly initially — instead, she was granted the sandals of Hermes, which gave her the ability to glide on air currents.
John Byrne's run on Wonder Woman (1995-1998) revealed that Hippolyta was the original Wonder Woman, having traveled back in time to fight in World War II with the Justice Society.
Phil Jimenez's Wonder Woman run (2001-2003) featured the 'Gods of Gotham' crossover, where Gotham City villains were empowered by rogue Olympian gods.
Allan Heinberg, the TV writer known for Grey's Anatomy, wrote a brief but well-received Wonder Woman run in 2006-2007.
J. Michael Straczynski's controversial 'Odyssey' storyline gave Wonder Woman a completely new origin and street-level costume with pants and a jacket.
"If it means interfering in an ensconced, archaic system, to help just one woman, man or child... I'm willing to accept the consequences." — Wonder Woman in the comics.
The New 52 Wonder Woman by Brian Azzarello ran for 35 issues and reimagined the Greek gods as modern, stylized figures — including Hades as a child with candle-wax for eyes.
Meredith Finch and David Finch took over Wonder Woman after Azzarello's New 52 run, though their tenure received more mixed reviews.
In DC Rebirth, Greg Rucka's 'The Lies' storyline revealed that Diana's memories of Themyscira were false, implanted to keep her from returning.
Tom King began writing Wonder Woman in 2023 with an epic run that saw Diana imprisoned and sentenced by a hostile U.S. government.
Tom King's Wonder Woman run explored themes of power, sovereignty, and motherhood, with Diana protecting a daughter named Trinity.
"The truth is powerful. I am powerful. You cannot stop what is coming." — Wonder Woman in Tom King's run.
Wonder Woman has been translated into dozens of languages and published in countries around the world, making her one of the most globally recognized superhero characters.
The Wonder Woman logo — the stylized 'WW' — has gone through numerous redesigns but remains one of the most iconic symbols in comics.
Wonder Woman's eagle-shaped breastplate was redesigned into a 'WW' logo by artist Alex Ross, creating a dual-meaning symbol that serves as both a uniform element and brand identity.
Wonder Woman has been featured on U.S. postage stamps multiple times, including a 2006 stamp and a 2016 stamp celebrating her 75th anniversary.
"You are stronger than you believe. You have greater power than you know." — a recurring theme in Wonder Woman stories across all media.
The 'Challenge of the Gods' storyline in Pérez's run sent Wonder Woman into a mythological underworld quest, battling creatures from Greek mythology.
In the 'War of the Gods' crossover (1991), Circe manipulated the pantheons of various mythologies into conflict, with Wonder Woman at the center of the crisis.
Wonder Woman once wielded the power of the Greek goddess Athena, temporarily becoming the Goddess of Truth.
During the 'Final Crisis' event, Wonder Woman was transformed into one of Darkseid's Female Furies before breaking free through sheer willpower.
In 'Kingdom Come' by Mark Waid and Alex Ross, Wonder Woman is depicted as a hardened warrior who has been expelled from Themyscira for failing her mission of peace.
Alex Ross's painted depiction of Wonder Woman in 'Kingdom Come' is considered one of the most beautiful and iconic renderings of the character.
"You want the truth? Everyone does, but can they handle it?" — Wonder Woman wielding the Lasso of Truth.
In 'The Dark Knight Returns' by Frank Miller, Wonder Woman makes a brief but memorable appearance as one of the few heroes still willing to fight alongside Batman.
Frank Miller later depicted Wonder Woman more controversially in 'All-Star Batman and Robin' and 'The Dark Knight Strikes Again,' where she was portrayed as aggressive and man-hating.
In the 'Elseworlds' story 'Amazonia' (1997), Wonder Woman is reimagined in a Victorian-era setting where she fights for women's suffrage.
The 'DC: The New Frontier' by Darwyn Cooke features a stunning depiction of Wonder Woman as a fierce Cold War-era warrior rescuing women from a Southeast Asian conflict.
"No man can tell an Amazon what she is or isn't. No man, no god, no one." — Wonder Woman in DC: The New Frontier.
In the 'Flashpoint' alternate timeline, Wonder Woman and the Amazons conquered the United Kingdom, renaming it New Themyscira and going to war with Aquaman's Atlantis.
The Flashpoint version of Wonder Woman was ruthless and violent, having beheaded Mera (Aquaman's wife) and wearing her crown as a trophy.
In 'Superman: Red Son,' which reimagines Superman landing in the Soviet Union, Wonder Woman is depicted as an ally of Superman who eventually turns against his regime.
The 'Legend of Wonder Woman' (2016) by Renae De Liz was a critically acclaimed retelling of Diana's origin set during World War II.
"But if you choose to leave, you may never return." — Hippolyta's warning to Diana about leaving Themyscira, a recurring element across multiple versions.
Joss Whedon was originally attached to write and direct a Wonder Woman film in 2005, but the project fell apart and he departed in 2007.
Before Gal Gadot, numerous actresses were rumored or considered for Wonder Woman over the years, including Cobie Smulders, Gina Carano, and Jamie Alexander.
Gal Gadot has stated that Wonder Woman's defining quality is her compassion, not her combat ability — she fights because she loves humanity.
"She was the best thing in Batman v Superman." — a widely repeated critical opinion about Gal Gadot's Wonder Woman debut.
The chemistry between Gal Gadot and Chris Pine was routinely praised by critics as one of the strengths of Wonder Woman (2017).
Wonder Woman (2017) was the first major superhero origin film set during World War I, a conflict rarely explored in the genre.
The village of Veld, where Diana liberates the townspeople in Wonder Woman (2017), was filmed on set in England and became one of the most memorable sequences in the film.
"I can't stand by while innocent lives are lost." — Diana, refusing to follow orders and charging into No Man's Land in Wonder Woman (2017).
The climactic battle between Diana and Ares in Wonder Woman (2017) takes place at an airfield, where Steve Trevor sacrifices himself to destroy Doctor Poison's gas bombs.
Steve Trevor's sacrifice — flying a plane loaded with poison gas to a safe altitude and detonating it — mirrors the self-sacrifice of classic war heroes.
"I wish we had more time. I love you." — Steve Trevor's last words to Diana before his sacrifice in Wonder Woman (2017).
Elena Anaya played Doctor Poison (Isabel Maru) in Wonder Woman (2017), a scarred chemist creating deadly gas weapons for the German army.
Saïd Taghmaoui's character Sameer in Wonder Woman (2017) is a Moroccan con man and aspiring actor who joins Trevor's mission behind enemy lines.
Ewen Bremner's character Charlie is a traumatized Scottish sharpshooter suffering from PTSD, adding emotional depth to the ragtag team in Wonder Woman (2017).
Eugene Brave Rock's character Chief Napi is a Blackfoot smuggler who points out to Diana that Steve Trevor's people took everything from his people — a powerful moment of nuance.
"The last war put him on the wrong side of history." — Chief, explaining why his people were displaced, in Wonder Woman (2017).
Wonder Woman has been depicted as bisexual in the comics, with Greg Rucka confirming in 2016 that Diana has had romantic relationships with women on Themyscira.
The relationship between Wonder Woman and Superman was a major storyline in the New 52 era, with the two dating and even starring in a joint comic series.
Wonder Woman's most enduring romantic relationship is with Steve Trevor, who has been her primary love interest since her very first appearance in 1941.
In addition to Steve Trevor and Superman, Wonder Woman has had romantic connections with Batman, Aquaman (in alternate timelines), and other characters.
"We have a mission. We are warriors. We are Amazons." — Queen Hippolyta rallying her warriors.
The Cheetah has undergone multiple incarnations — Priscilla Rich (Golden Age), Deborah Domaine (Silver Age), Barbara Ann Minerva (modern era), and Sebastian Ballesteros.
Barbara Ann Minerva became the Cheetah after a ritual involving an ancient plant god, giving her superhuman speed, agility, and ferocity — but also cursing her.
In Greg Rucka's Rebirth run, Barbara Minerva and Diana were close friends before Barbara's transformation into the Cheetah, adding tragedy to their rivalry.
Circe is an immortal sorceress from Greek mythology who serves as one of Wonder Woman's most dangerous magical foes, capable of transforming people into animals.
Veronica Cale, a wealthy businesswoman and rival of Diana, was introduced in the comics as a foil who opposes Wonder Woman through manipulation rather than physical combat.
"I don't understand hatred between people. I truly don't." — Wonder Woman, expressing her fundamental belief in compassion.
Doctor Psycho is a misogynistic telepathic villain and one of Wonder Woman's earliest foes, representing the patriarchal forces the character was created to oppose.
Silver Swan is a recurring Wonder Woman villain — a woman transformed into a powerful, swan-themed metahuman, whose story often explores themes of beauty and self-worth.
The First Born, introduced in the New 52, is the firstborn son of Zeus and Hera who was abandoned and raised in the wild, becoming a brutal antagonist for Wonder Woman.
Devastation is a clay creature created by the Titan Cronus as an evil counterpart to Wonder Woman, possessing similar powers but driven by destruction.
"The Gods of Olympus have abandoned this world, and the weapons of war are all that remain to us." — from Wonder Woman comic lore.
In the 'Sacrifice' storyline, Maxwell Lord used his telepathic powers to control Superman, forcing Wonder Woman to make the horrific choice of killing Lord to save the world.
Wonder Woman's decision to kill Maxwell Lord was broadcast worldwide, turning public opinion against her and forcing her into hiding — a pivotal moment in DC continuity.
The Trinity War crossover featured Wonder Woman as a central character, with tensions between the Justice League, Justice League of America, and Justice League Dark.
Wonder Woman led the Justice League Dark for a period, combining her mystical background with the team's supernatural focus.
"This is not about fighting for a country or a government. This is about fighting for what's right." — Wonder Woman's core principle.
In the 'Dead Earth' miniseries (2020), Wonder Woman awakens in a post-apocalyptic future and must rebuild civilization while confronting mutated survivors.
The 'Historia: The Amazons' graphic novel by Kelly Sue DeConnick explored the mythological origins of the Amazons across three chapters spanning thousands of years.
The 'Sensation Comics Featuring Wonder Woman' digital-first series (2014-2016) featured standalone Wonder Woman stories by various creative teams.
Wonder Woman: Earth One by Grant Morrison and Yanick Paquette presented a modernized Elseworlds origin that leaned into Marston's themes of loving submission and female empowerment.
Grant Morrison described their Wonder Woman: Earth One as striving to recapture the 'bizarre, brilliant sexuality' of Marston's original vision.
"In loving submission, strength." — a recurring theme from William Moulton Marston's original Wonder Woman philosophy.
The animated series 'Justice League Action' (2016-2018) featured Rachel Kimsey as the voice of Wonder Woman in fun, action-packed shorts.
Wonder Woman appeared in the animated film 'Batman: The Brave and the Bold' and has been featured in multiple DC animated movie universe entries.
In the animated film 'Wonder Woman: Bloodlines' (2019), Rosario Dawson voiced Diana in a story spanning her early days to a confrontation with multiple villains.
Rosario Dawson also voiced Wonder Woman in the DC Animated Movie Universe series of interconnected films.
Wonder Woman has been a staple character in Super Smash Bros.-style fighting games, including MultiVersus, where she is a tank-class fighter.
"The strong one is not the one who wins. The strong one is the one who doesn't give up." — Wonder Woman, a sentiment frequently attributed to the character.
Wonder Woman's bullet-deflecting bracelets scene — where she crosses her arms and deflects projectiles — has become one of the most widely imitated superhero poses.
The 'bracelets' moment in Wonder Woman (2017), where Diana deflects gunfire for the first time in No Man's Land, was preceded by a slow-motion sequence that became instantly iconic.
Wonder Woman's comic book has had numerous variant covers by some of the industry's top artists, including Adam Hughes, Artgerm, and Jenny Frison.
Adam Hughes became famous for his stunning Wonder Woman covers, drawing the character with elegance and power for years.
Jenny Frison's Wonder Woman covers during the Rebirth era were praised for their ethereal beauty and emotional depth.
"What matters is what you do with the truth once you have it." — Wonder Woman, on the responsibility that comes with knowledge.
Wonder Woman was a central character in the 'Infinite Crisis' event (2005-2006), one of DC's biggest crossover events.
In 'Infinite Crisis,' Wonder Woman's killing of Maxwell Lord plays a pivotal role in fracturing the trust between Superman, Batman, and herself — the DC Trinity.
The aftermath of 'Infinite Crisis' saw Wonder Woman step back from heroics for a year, adopting the Diana Prince identity and working as an agent for the Department of Metahuman Affairs.
Wonder Woman has served as the God of War in the comics, inheriting the title after Ares' death during the New 52 era.
As the God of War, Wonder Woman struggled to balance her peaceful nature with the violent instincts that came with the title.
"Even in the darkest of times, there is always hope. Sometimes you just need to look harder." — Wonder Woman in the comics.
The Amazons have been depicted differently across various comic runs — sometimes as peaceful scholars, other times as fierce isolationist warriors.
In the New 52, the Amazons were controversially depicted as seducing and killing sailors, then enslaving their male children — a portrayal later retconned.
Philippus is a prominent Amazon warrior and captain of the guard on Themyscira, often depicted as Hippolyta's closest advisor and, in some versions, her romantic partner.
Io is an Amazonian blacksmith who forged many of Wonder Woman's weapons and was depicted as having unrequited romantic feelings for Diana.
Mala is one of Diana's closest Amazon friends on Themyscira, who competed against Diana in the original tournament to leave the island.
"Sisters in arms, fighting for a world that may never fully understand us." — a sentiment shared among the Amazons.
Steve Trevor has died and been resurrected multiple times throughout DC Comics history, making his relationship with Diana one of the most enduring and complicated in comics.
In some versions of the story, Steve Trevor is an Air Force pilot rather than Army intelligence, though his role as Diana's link to 'Man's World' remains consistent.
The 2017 film portrays Steve Trevor as a World War I spy working for British intelligence, a departure from his traditional military role.
Chris Pine improvised several humorous moments in Wonder Woman (2017), including the bath scene where Steve tries to explain his watch to Diana.
"A watch? You let that little thing tell you what to do?" — Diana to Steve Trevor about his watch, in Wonder Woman (2017).
The London scenes in Wonder Woman (2017), where Diana tries on early 20th-century clothing with Etta Candy, were inspired by classic fish-out-of-water comedy.
Wonder Woman's shield has become as iconic as Captain America's in the rival Marvel universe, both serving as symbols of their respective characters' protective nature.
In some comics, Wonder Woman carries the Aegis of Athena, a divine shield blessed by the goddess that provides supernatural protection.
"If you see something wrong happening in the world, you can either do nothing or you can do something. And I already tried nothing." — Wonder Woman in the comics.
Wonder Woman's fighting style in the DCEU films incorporates elements of Krav Maga, kung fu, and sword fighting, designed by fight coordinator Eunice Huthart.
Gal Gadot trained for nine months before filming Wonder Woman (2017), gaining 17 pounds of muscle for the role.
The Amazons' fighting style in the films was designed to look distinct from other cinematic warriors, incorporating gymnastics and horseback combat.
Robin Wright, who played Antiope, was 50 years old during filming of Wonder Woman (2017) and underwent months of intensive physical training.
"Fighting does not make you a hero. What you fight for does." — General Antiope in Wonder Woman lore.
Wonder Woman's first meeting with Batman in the comics has been depicted differently across continuities, but their mutual respect is a constant.
The Batman/Wonder Woman romantic tension has been explored in animated series, comics, and alternate universes, though their mainstream relationship remains platonic.
In the 'Justice League' animated series, the romantic subplot between Batman and Wonder Woman included the memorable 'this doesn't change anything' kiss.
Wonder Woman and Superman's relationship in the New 52 comics explored what would happen if two of the most powerful beings in the universe fell in love.
The Superman/Wonder Woman New 52 series ran for 29 issues from 2013 to 2016 before being ended with the DC Rebirth relaunch.
"We're not gods, we're not monsters. We're just people trying to do the right thing." — Wonder Woman to Superman in the comics.
Wonder Woman has been featured in psychological studies examining how female superhero representation affects girls' self-confidence and body image.
Research published in the journal 'Sex Roles' found that exposure to Wonder Woman increased women's belief in their own strength and capability.
Wonder Woman costumes are consistently among the top-selling Halloween costumes for girls and women every year.
The 2017 film's success led to a significant increase in baby girls being named Diana in the United States.
"Power without compassion is tyranny. Compassion without power is merely pity." — Wonder Woman in the comics.
Artist George Pérez drew inspiration from his own Puerto Rican heritage when designing his version of Wonder Woman, giving her a more Mediterranean appearance.
The Wonder Woman Museum in Stamford, Connecticut, operated by the Marston family, houses original artwork and memorabilia from the character's early years.
Jill Thompson's manga-inspired 'Wonder Woman: The True Amazon' graphic novel (2016) offered a unique retelling of Diana's youth on Themyscira.
The Eisner Award-winning 'DC: The New Frontier' by Darwyn Cooke features one of the most powerful Wonder Woman sequences ever drawn, showing her liberating captive women in conflict.
"No one deserves to suffer. That is my credo. That is what drives me." — Wonder Woman in the comics.
Nicola Scott co-illustrated Greg Rucka's Wonder Woman: Year One, creating one of the most visually beautiful Wonder Woman origin stories ever published.
Liam Sharp illustrated the present-day 'The Lies' storyline in Rucka's Rebirth run, contrasting with Nicola Scott's 'Year One' flashback art style.
Cliff Chiang's art on the New 52 Wonder Woman was praised for its clean, bold style that gave the Greek gods a modern, almost fashion-forward aesthetic.
In Cliff Chiang's depiction, Hera was drawn as a peacock-themed figure with feathered elements, while Apollo was depicted with dark sun-scorched skin.
"Gods are real. And they are not kind." — Wonder Woman's understanding of the divine in the New 52.
The Man of Steel crossover between Superman and Wonder Woman comics saw both heroes dealing with the consequences of revealing their relationship to the world.
Wonder Woman once wielded a Black Lantern ring during the Blackest Night event before a Star Sapphire ring replaced it, drawn to her capacity for love.
In 'Forever Evil,' Wonder Woman fought against the Crime Syndicate's invasion of Earth, battling her evil counterpart Superwoman.
Superwoman, Wonder Woman's counterpart in the Crime Syndicate, is the evil version of Lois Lane on Earth-3, combining beauty with ruthless cruelty.
"The measure of a hero is not in their fists, but in their heart." — Wonder Woman, embodying her ethos of compassion over violence.
The 2020 'Death Metal' event featured Wonder Woman as the central hero, wielding the chainsaw of truth and leading the resistance against the Batman Who Laughs.
In 'Dark Nights: Death Metal,' Wonder Woman becomes the 'Last Knight on Earth,' the final hope of the multiverse against cosmic-level threats.
Wonder Woman has wielded multiple magical artifacts throughout her history, including the Gauntlet of Atlas, which multiplies her strength tenfold.
The Sandals of Hermes, granted to Wonder Woman by the messenger god in George Pérez's run, allowed her to travel at superhuman speed.
"I fight for those who cannot fight for themselves. It's not just what I do — it's who I am." — Wonder Woman in the comics.
Wonder Woman's Golden Perfect lasso is one of the most powerful objects in the DC Universe, said to be an extension of truth itself.
In some stories, the Lasso of Truth can restore lost memories, cure madness, and even reveal a person's true self by burning away illusions.
The Lasso of Truth has been broken on rare occasions, usually by cosmic-level events or beings of extreme power, and its destruction is always treated as catastrophic.
Wonder Woman carries a pair of 'Amazonian' short swords in many modern depictions, reflecting her warrior heritage more than her diplomatic mission.
"I will never stop fighting for what is right. That is my oath." — Wonder Woman in DC Comics.
Patty Jenkins drew on the work of Richard Donner's Superman (1978) as a key influence when crafting the tone and sincerity of Wonder Woman (2017).
The alley scene in Wonder Woman (2017) where Diana saves Steve Trevor from German spies in London was partly inspired by Christopher Reeve's Superman saving Lois Lane in a Metropolis alley.
Patty Jenkins has cited the 'No Man's Land' scene as the 'birth of a hero' moment, comparable to Superman's first flight or Spider-Man's first web-swing.
Wonder Woman (2017) features a diverse supporting cast representing different nationalities and backgrounds, unusual for a superhero blockbuster set during WWI.
"What I do is not up to you." — Diana Prince to Steve Trevor in Wonder Woman (2017), asserting her independence.
Wonder Woman (2017) earned $412 million domestically and $821 million worldwide, making it one of the top-grossing films of 2017.
Wonder Woman (2017) holds the record for the highest-grossing film by a solo female director (Patty Jenkins) without a male co-directing credit.
Wonder Woman was the first DCEU film to receive overwhelmingly positive reviews, after Man of Steel, Batman v Superman, and Suicide Squad received more mixed reception.
The success of Wonder Woman (2017) paved the way for more female-led superhero films, including Captain Marvel (2019) and Black Widow (2021).
"Where I come from, generals don't hide in their offices. They fight alongside their soldiers. They die with their soldiers." — Diana to the British generals in Wonder Woman (2017).
Wonder Woman 1984 features an extended sequence where Diana learns to fly for the first time, riding air currents inspired by Steve Trevor's lesson about wind.
The White House confrontation scene in Wonder Woman 1984 features Diana in her full armor battling Secret Service agents to reach Maxwell Lord.
Pedro Pascal based his portrayal of Maxwell Lord partly on real-life 1980s figures like Donald Trump and televangelist Jim Bakker.
Kristen Wiig's transformation into Cheetah in Wonder Woman 1984 follows a three-stage arc: mousy scientist, empowered socialite, then full feline supervillain.
"I don't want to be like anyone. I want to be an apex predator." — Barbara Minerva as she transforms into Cheetah in Wonder Woman 1984.
Wonder Woman 1984 was one of the first major Hollywood films to be released simultaneously on a streaming platform (HBO Max) and in theaters.
The decision to release Wonder Woman 1984 on HBO Max was controversial, with Patty Jenkins later expressing disappointment that the film didn't get a full theatrical run.
Gal Gadot performed many of her own fight choreography sequences in both Wonder Woman films, including wire work and fight training.
The lasso-swinging sequences in the Wonder Woman films used a combination of practical rope work and CGI enhancement.
"I am Diana of Themyscira. I have no fear." — Wonder Woman in various DC media.
Wonder Woman's immune system is far superior to any human's; she is resistant to poison, disease, and most forms of biological attack.
In some comic runs, Wonder Woman can communicate with animals, a power granted by the goddess Artemis.
Wonder Woman possesses the Wisdom of Athena, a divine gift that grants her tactical genius and vast knowledge.
The Blessing of Demeter grants Wonder Woman enhanced connection to the earth and boosts her already formidable durability.
Hermes' blessing grants Wonder Woman superhuman speed — in the comics, she has been shown to move fast enough to deflect automatic gunfire from multiple directions simultaneously.
"War is not noble. It is not glorious. But sometimes it is necessary." — Wonder Woman, reflecting on conflict.
Aphrodite's blessing grants Wonder Woman incredible beauty and a loving heart, but also makes her understand and empathize deeply with human emotion.
Wonder Woman has been shown to bench press the Earth in some comic continuities, putting her strength roughly on par with Superman.
Diana can survive in the vacuum of space without a suit for extended periods, something she has demonstrated in several Justice League storylines.
Wonder Woman's speed has been clocked at FTL (faster than light) levels when she pushes herself to her absolute limits.
"Sisters, the time has come to stand and fight. Protect Themyscira!" — Hippolyta rallying the Amazons in battle.
In the DC animated film 'Superman/Batman: Apocalypse' (2010), Wonder Woman plays a key role in training Kara Zor-El (Supergirl) on Themyscira.
Wonder Woman fought Doomsday — the creature that killed Superman — and held her own, demonstrating her incredible power level.
In 'The OMAC Project,' Wonder Woman's killing of Maxwell Lord triggered the creation of an army of cyborg soldiers designed to eliminate metahumans.
The Brother Eye satellite, created by Batman and hijacked by Maxwell Lord, turned ordinary humans into OMACs — a threat Wonder Woman helped neutralize.
"It is our sacred duty to defend the world. And it's what I'm going to do." — Wonder Woman in Batman v Superman: Dawn of Justice (2016).
The Wonder Woman letters column in the comics, called 'From the Desk of Diana Prince' in some eras, featured passionate fan correspondence about the character.
Wonder Woman has been parodied and referenced in numerous TV shows, including Seinfeld, The Big Bang Theory, Family Guy, and Saturday Night Live.
Lynda Carter has been inducted into several halls of fame and continues to be associated with Wonder Woman, making appearances at conventions and events.
Lynda Carter sang the theme for the Wonder Woman TV series and has released multiple music albums throughout her career.
"The Gods see everything, Diana." — Hippolyta's cautionary words to her daughter in multiple adaptations.
The Wonder Woman franchise has generated billions in merchandise sales, including action figures, clothing, jewelry, and home goods.
Wonder Woman LEGO sets have been popular sellers, featuring recreations of scenes from both the comics and films.
Hot Toys, Sideshow Collectibles, and other premium figure makers have released highly detailed Wonder Woman collectibles based on Gal Gadot's likeness.
The 2017 Wonder Woman Barbie doll was one of the best-selling Barbie dolls of that year.
"I am willing to fight for those who cannot fight for themselves, even when the odds are against me." — Wonder Woman in the comics.
Wonder Woman has appeared in nearly every major DC crossover event, from Crisis on Infinite Earths to Doomsday Clock to Dark Crisis.
In 'Doomsday Clock' by Geoff Johns, Wonder Woman plays a role in the collision between the DC Universe and the Watchmen universe.
In 'Dark Crisis on Infinite Earths' (2022), Wonder Woman was among the heroes believed dead, having been trapped in a dark dimension by Pariah.
Wonder Woman has appeared in multiple 'Elseworlds' stories, which reimagine DC characters in alternate settings and time periods.
In 'Wonder Woman: The Blue Amazon,' Diana is reimagined as a robot created by a female inventor, exploring themes of artificial intelligence and humanity.
"We women are the nurturers, the caregivers, the backbone of civilization. But never confuse that with weakness." — Wonder Woman in the comics.
In 'Bombshells,' set during WWII, Wonder Woman leads a diverse team of superheroines inspired by pinup art, fighting fascism alongside Batwoman and Supergirl.
'DC Bombshells' reinvented Wonder Woman as a resistance fighter who recruits women from various nations to join the war effort against the Axis powers.
Wonder Woman has appeared in the 'Injustice' comic series, which serves as a prequel to the video games, exploring her descent into authoritarianism alongside Superman.
In the Injustice comics, Wonder Woman's loyalty to Superman's regime is partly motivated by her romantic feelings for him, adding moral complexity to her character.
"Gods do not compromise." — Wonder Woman in the Injustice comics, justifying her alliance with Superman's authoritarian rule.
The Injustice 2 version of Wonder Woman is one of the most powerful characters in the game, wielding her lasso, sword, and shield in devastating combos.
Wonder Woman has been depicted in manga-style artwork through DC's collaboration with Japanese publishers, reimagining her for East Asian audiences.
DC's 'Manga Wonder Woman' one-shot included stories by Japanese artists, showing how the character translates across cultural artistic traditions.
Wonder Woman: Warbringer by Leigh Bardugo is a young adult novel exploring Diana's teenage years and her first encounter with the human world.
"To be an Amazon, you must first learn to fight. To be a hero, you must first learn to love." — a paraphrased lesson from Amazonian philosophy.
Diana's golden tiara has a red star in its center in her classic design, symbolizing her role as the Amazons' chosen champion.
Wonder Woman's classic red, blue, and gold color scheme was inspired by the American flag, aligning the character with wartime patriotism.
The eagle emblem on Wonder Woman's bustier was later redesigned as a 'WW' symbol, with the double-W cleverly forming the eagle's wing shape.
Wonder Woman's costume has been redesigned dozens of times, with each era's artists putting their own stamp on her iconic look.
"The truth is not always pretty, but it is always necessary." — Wonder Woman wielding the Lasso of Truth.
In the 'Blackest Night' event, Wonder Woman fought zombified versions of fallen heroes and villains before being temporarily claimed by the Star Sapphire Corps.
Wonder Woman's compassion is so powerful that she was chosen by the Star Sapphire ring, which seeks out those with the greatest capacity for love.
In 'Trinity' (2008-2009) by Kurt Busiek, Wonder Woman, Superman, and Batman are shown to be metaphysically linked, each representing a fundamental aspect of heroism.
The 'Trinity' series explored what would happen if Wonder Woman, Superman, and Batman were removed from the world, revealing how essential each is to the DC Universe.
"Truth, justice, and compassion — those are my weapons, and I wield them without compromise." — Wonder Woman.
Wonder Woman Annual #1 (2017) featured a story where Diana and a young girl in a refugee camp bonded over shared experiences of leaving home.
The 'Rebirth' era gave Wonder Woman twin brother Jason, a demigod son of Hippolyta and Zeus, whose introduction was divisive among fans.
Grail, the daughter of Darkseid, was introduced as a major antagonist for Wonder Woman, being born on the same day as Diana.
In Geoff Johns' 'Darkseid War,' Wonder Woman temporarily became the God of Death, inheriting the power of the Black Racer.
"I have learned that you cannot judge people by where they come from. Only by what they do." — Wonder Woman in the comics.
Wonder Woman's Rebirth run alternated between present-day and origin storylines in odd and even numbered issues, creating a unique narrative structure.
The 'Year One' arc in Rebirth showed Diana's wide-eyed wonder at discovering modern civilization for the first time, including her amazement at ice cream.
Diana's traditional greeting of offering her wrists to be bound is a sign of trust among Amazons, symbolizing voluntary submission and peaceful intent.
In several comic continuities, the Amazons' immortality is tied to Themyscira itself — leaving the island means eventually aging and dying like mortals.
"I came to this world with an open heart, and it was nearly broken. But I never let it close." — Wonder Woman on her experiences in Man's World.
Wonder Woman's arch-nemesis Cheetah has appeared in multiple animated adaptations, including Justice League, Justice League Unlimited, and DC Super Hero Girls.
In the Justice League Unlimited animated series, the Cheetah was voiced by Sheryl Lee Ralph and later Morena Baccarin.
The animated Wonder Woman (2009) film featured a particularly brutal and satisfying fight between Diana and Ares, ending with Diana beheading the god of war.
'Wonder Woman: Bloodlines' (2019) condensed multiple comic storylines into a single animated film, featuring Cheetah, Doctor Poison, Giganta, Silver Swan, and Medusa.
"I cannot stand by while the world falls apart. It is not in my nature." — Wonder Woman in animated adaptations.
Wonder Woman has appeared in both the Mortal Kombat vs. DC Universe crossover game and the Injustice games, fighting alongside and against characters from both franchises.
In Mortal Kombat vs. DC Universe, Wonder Woman fights Kitana in a memorable crossover matchup between two iconic female warriors.
The announced Wonder Woman video game by Monolith Productions is expected to use the Nemesis System, which dynamically creates unique enemy relationships.
Wonder Woman has appeared in the mobile game Legends of Tomorrow and has been featured as a summonable character in various DC mobile titles.
"This world was a beautiful place. I want it to be beautiful again." — Wonder Woman in the Injustice game series.
The Professor Marston and the Wonder Women (2017) film, starring Luke Evans, Rebecca Hall, and Bella Heathcote, dramatized the real-life relationship behind Wonder Woman's creation.
The film received mixed reviews but was praised for its performances and its exploration of the unconventional Marston household.
Angela Robinson directed Professor Marston and the Wonder Women, drawing on extensive research into the Marston family's actual life.
Elizabeth Holloway Marston outlived both William Marston and Olive Byrne, passing away in 1993 at the age of 100.
Olive Byrne's bracelets — the inspiration for Wonder Woman's Bracelets of Submission — were actually cuff-style bracelets she wore as part of her personal style.
"Frankly, Wonder Woman is psychological propaganda for the new type of woman who should, I believe, rule the world." — William Moulton Marston in a 1943 interview.
Margaret Sanger, the birth control activist, was Olive Byrne's aunt and a major influence on the feminist ideals embedded in Wonder Woman.
Marston's original Wonder Woman stories featured the 'Holiday Girls,' a group of college sorority women who assisted Diana — reflecting Marston's ideas about female solidarity.
Etta Candy in the original comics was a stout, fearless woman who led the Holiday Girls into battle with nothing but a box of candy and sheer determination.
The original Wonder Woman comics are now public domain in some countries, leading to various independent reinterpretations of the early stories.
"Suffering builds character. But so does love." — Wonder Woman-inspired sentiment from Marston's philosophy.
Wonder Woman was featured in DC's 'Year of the Villain' event, facing threats amplified by Lex Luthor's offer of power to the world's villains.
In 'Future State' (2021), Wonder Woman stars in a dystopian future story where the Amazons face an undead threat to Themyscira.
Yara Flor, a Brazilian Amazon, was introduced in 'Future State' as a potential new Wonder Woman, representing a diverse future for the franchise.
Nubia was given her own miniseries, 'Nubia and the Amazons' (2021), and crowned queen of Themyscira, expanding the Amazonian mythology.
"The future belongs to those who believe in the beauty of their dreams." — a sentiment shared among the Amazons of various generations.
Wonder Woman's comic series reached issue #800 in 2023, making it one of the longest-running superhero comics featuring a female protagonist.
Tom King's Wonder Woman run, which began with issue #1 in 2023, was structured as a courtroom drama, with Diana on trial by a government tribunal.
In Tom King's run, the Amazons declare war on the United States after Diana is unjustly imprisoned, creating a geopolitical crisis.
Daniel Sampere's art on Tom King's Wonder Woman received widespread acclaim for its detailed, emotionally expressive depictions of Diana.
"I am no man's weapon. I am no god's puppet. I am Wonder Woman." — Diana's defiant declaration in the comics.
Wonder Woman's 80th anniversary in 2021 was celebrated with special editions, merchandise, and a dedicated DC FanDome panel.
DC released a special Wonder Woman 80th Anniversary Super Spectacular one-shot featuring stories by multiple creative teams.
The Library of Congress held a Wonder Woman exhibition in 2017 to coincide with the character's 75th anniversary and the release of the film.
Wonder Woman is one of the most cosplayed characters at comic conventions worldwide, with elaborate handmade costumes being a staple at events like San Diego Comic-Con.
"I believe in the inherent goodness of people. That is not naive — it is the bravest thing I can do." — Wonder Woman in the comics.
In several alternate-future stories, Diana has a daughter who inherits the Wonder Woman mantle, continuing the legacy of the Amazons.
The concept of 'legacy' is central to Wonder Woman's mythology — from Hippolyta to Diana to Donna Troy to Cassie Sandsmark, the title passes through generations.
Wonder Woman's tiara throw — removing her tiara and hurling it like a discus — is one of her signature combat moves in both comics and animation.
The Lasso of Truth glows golden when activated and is sometimes depicted as being warm to the touch, symbolizing the comforting nature of honesty.
"Man has always feared what he doesn't understand. But understanding is the first step toward acceptance." — Wonder Woman in the comics.
Ares in the post-Crisis comics by George Pérez was depicted not in traditional Greek armor but in a dark, monstrous form reflecting the horrors of modern warfare.
In Greg Rucka's Rebirth, Ares was reimagined as a weary, chained god who actually mentored Diana rather than opposed her, a significant departure from tradition.
The Circle, a group of four Amazon guards — Alkyone, Myrto, Charis, and Philomela — served as compelling antagonists during Gail Simone's run.
Veronica Cale was reimagined in Rebirth as the head of Godwatch, an organization devoted to finding Themyscira, making her one of the most complex modern Wonder Woman villains.
"Knowledge without wisdom is meaningless. Power without purpose is dangerous." — Wonder Woman in the comics.
Wonder Woman's sword, in various continuities, has been named Godkiller, Athena's Blade, and simply the Amazonian Sword — each with different properties.
The Bracelets of Submission were forged from the remnants of Athena's legendary shield, the Aegis, in George Pérez's continuity.
If Wonder Woman's bracelets are removed, she enters a berserker rage, unleashing the full uncontrolled power of the Amazons — a concept used in several storylines.
In the animated Justice League series, Wonder Woman was briefly turned to stone by a sorcerer, with Batman memorably cradling her statue form.
"The world of men is no place for a child, Diana." — Hippolyta's concern for her daughter in countless adaptations.
Wonder Woman has traveled through time on multiple occasions, visiting ancient Greece, World War II, and even far-future timelines.
In one of her earliest time-travel stories, Wonder Woman met a younger version of her mother Hippolyta in ancient Greece.
The Amazons celebrate 'Feast Day' on Themyscira — a day commemorating their freedom from bondage, shown in several comic runs.
Themyscira has been depicted as everything from a simple island paradise to a technologically advanced hidden civilization, depending on the era.
"Home is not a place. It is wherever the people you love are." — Diana's reflection after leaving Themyscira.
In the 2017 film, Themyscira is protected by a magical barrier that renders it invisible to the outside world — a concept drawn from the comics.
The Amazons in the 2017 film speak a variety of languages, reflecting the idea that they are drawn from civilizations throughout history.
Wonder Woman is proficient in virtually every human language, thanks to the Blessing of Athena, which grants her linguistic abilities.
In Batman v Superman, Bruce Wayne discovers a photograph of Diana from 1918, revealing that she has not aged in nearly a century.
"A hundred years ago I walked away from mankind. From a century of horrors. Men made a world where standing together is impossible." — Diana in Batman v Superman (2016).
Wonder Woman was featured in the animated 'DC Showcase' short film collection, with a segment set in ancient times showing her battling mythological creatures.
In the 'Batman: The Brave and the Bold' animated series, Wonder Woman appeared in a memorable episode that explored Amazonian customs and culture.
Wonder Woman: Spirit of Truth (2001) by Paul Dini and Alex Ross is a beautifully painted one-shot exploring Diana's attempts to help the real world beyond superhero battles.
Alex Ross's realistic painted art made Wonder Woman look like a classical sculpture come to life in Spirit of Truth, elevating the character's visual presentation.
"The truth is the truth, no matter how much you try to hide from it." — Wonder Woman wielding the Lasso of Truth.
The 'Wonder Woman by George Pérez Omnibus' is considered essential reading for any fan of the character, collecting his entire legendary run.
Greg Rucka's first Wonder Woman run has been collected as 'Wonder Woman by Greg Rucka' in trade paperbacks that remain in print and widely read.
The 'Wonder Woman: Year One' Rebirth collection by Rucka and Nicola Scott is often recommended as the best introduction to the character for new readers.
Gail Simone's Wonder Woman run has been collected in trade paperbacks titled 'The Circle,' 'Ends of the Earth,' 'Rise of the Olympian,' and 'Warkiller.'
"I am not here to be a symbol. I am here to do what is right." — Wonder Woman in the comics.
Wonder Woman's popularity surged dramatically after the 2017 film, with comic book sales, merchandise, and overall brand awareness reaching new heights.
The success of Wonder Woman (2017) proved that female-led superhero films could be massive box office hits, dispelling a long-held Hollywood myth.
Patty Jenkins was the first woman to direct a film that earned over $800 million worldwide when Wonder Woman crossed that milestone.
Wonder Woman (2017) was selected by the National Board of Review as one of the top ten films of 2017.
"I used to want to save the world, this beautiful place. But I knew so little then." — Diana's opening narration in Wonder Woman (2017).
The musical score of Wonder Woman (2017), composed by Rupert Gregson-Williams, incorporated Diana's now-iconic electric cello theme throughout.
The 'Is She With You?' track from the Batman v Superman soundtrack, featuring Wonder Woman's theme, became a viral sensation and crowd-pleasing concert piece.
Tina Guo, the cellist who performed Wonder Woman's theme, has spoken about how the piece changed her career and brought classical instruments into superhero culture.
Wonder Woman 1984 was the first major Hollywood film to adopt the day-and-date streaming release model, influencing theatrical distribution strategies going forward.
"I learned that nothing good is born from lies, Steve. Nothing." — Diana in Wonder Woman 1984.
The '80s aesthetic of Wonder Woman 1984 included deliberately bright, oversaturated colors and period-accurate costume and set design.
The opening Amazonian Games sequence in Wonder Woman 1984 was designed to evoke the ancient Greek Olympics, with young Diana competing against adult Amazons.
Wonder Woman 3 was announced and then cancelled during the restructuring of DC Studios under James Gunn and Peter Safran in late 2022.
James Gunn confirmed that Wonder Woman would appear in the new DC Universe but did not confirm Gal Gadot's involvement in the rebooted continuity.
"Every century, Amazons watch the world grow more dangerous. But it is in the moments of crisis that heroes are born." — Amazonian wisdom in DC Comics.
Wonder Woman consistently ranks as one of the most recognizable fictional characters in the world, alongside Superman, Batman, and Spider-Man.
Surveys have shown that Wonder Woman's 'W' logo is one of the most recognized symbols globally, even among people who don't read comics.
Wonder Woman-themed running events and marathons have been organized worldwide, celebrating the character's ethos of strength and perseverance.
DC has published Wonder Woman coloring books, activity books, and early reader books aimed at introducing the character to the youngest audiences.
"Never forget: you are braver than you believe, stronger than you seem, and smarter than you think." — sentiments echoed across Wonder Woman media.
Wonder Woman appeared on a 2016 United Nations stamp, part of a UN Postal Administration set featuring DC superheroes promoting gender equality.
The 75th anniversary of Wonder Woman in 2016 was marked by a new DC Comics logo, commemorative editions, and the UN honorary ambassador appointment.
Wonder Woman's enduring popularity for over 80 years makes her the longest-running female superhero in comic book history with continuous publication.
DC has released numerous 'Essential' and 'Greatest Stories Ever Told' collections focused on Wonder Woman, curating her best adventures across decades.
"The world needs more heroes. Not because we lack strength, but because we must choose to use it for good." — Wonder Woman in the comics.
In 'Kingdom Come,' Wonder Woman wears armor and carries a sword, foreshadowing the more warrior-focused depictions that became standard in the 2010s and beyond.
Artist J. Scott Campbell's variant covers for Wonder Woman have been popular collector's items, showcasing a stylized, dynamic take on Diana.
Terry Dodson's art on Wonder Woman from 2006 to 2007 brought a classic Hollywood glamour to the character that many fans adored.
Mike Deodato Jr.'s 1990s Wonder Woman art featured highly detailed action sequences and redesigned the character with a more modern, aggressive look.
"Every woman is a Wonder Woman. Some just haven't discovered their tiara yet." — a popular sentiment inspired by the character.
Wonder Woman's influence extends beyond comics into fashion, with designers like Valentino and Diane von Furstenberg creating Wonder Woman-inspired collections.
The Met Gala has seen multiple celebrities wear Wonder Woman-inspired outfits, cementing the character's status as a fashion icon.
Wonder Woman has been referenced in songs, with Beyoncé, Katy Perry, and other artists citing the character as an inspiration for female empowerment.
Gal Gadot has spoken about how playing Wonder Woman changed her life, making her a role model for millions and giving her a platform to advocate for gender equality.
